title: be going to (do)
desc: 계획된 의도 또는 근거 있는 예측
examples: And if your front porch light is on, that means you have candy and you're handing it out. If it's off, that either means you ran out of candy or you are not home. So, we are going to have a lot of fun eating all of Wes's candy apparently. So, come get ready for our first Halloween with us. Wes was going to dress up as Chucky the demon child, but he outgrew his overalls. So, we are going to wear a Halloween themed shirt this year, and next year we'll get him a proper costume. There you go.

We had some trouble getting it over his head because honestly, I have never put anything over his head thus far. It's always went from the bottom up. But we are ready in our costume. We're going to put some pants on and then we're going to go trick-or-treating.

Yeah. Oh, we're excited. So, before we head out for trick-or-treating, I am going to feed him his dinner of chicken and green beans. In my town, when you hear the tornado siren going off, that means trick-or-treating has started.
>> I was a green dinosaur when I was four years old. He's going to wear it.
>> You're going to be a dino when you're four.

title: will / shall
examples: He's still a little young, so trick-or-treating might not go as planned, but we're at least going to walk around the neighborhood and let him people watch and see everybody in their costumes. This brings back a lot of memories for me of getting dressed up with my sister, running around our neighborhood, getting candy, and staying out a little bit later, and then coming home and sorting it all. In America, the traditions can vary greatly, but most of the time, people will hand out candy.

title: should (advice)
examples: Do you think we should change him into a sloth?
